UML:TaggedValue.value:
The Strategy package (and Initiative package) represent potential or planned change in a business. The Value and Capability packages represent abstract modeling perspectives on the business that are useful for 1) consolidating groups of activities the significantly contribute value to a product, or 2) consolidating similar structures and/or behaviors occurring in multiple parts of the business. The other packages represent business concepts that are often called the "operating model" of the business.
Only the operating model concepts of a business are subject to change resulting from a strategy or an initiative. The Value and Capability concepts are used only for model analysis and presentation; trying to change them will not produce any change in the underlying business.
It does not make sense in a static model (one that is not intended for execution or simulation) to represent a strategy that intends to change itself or another strategy. Having a strategy model that can change itself implies that the meaning of the model cannot be statically determined.
An OrgChart structure is a hierarchical command and control structure.

The DefinesOrgChartSegment association class designates the people in each BusinessUnit associated with an instance of an ORGChartStructure. The OrgChartStructure must partition the Business so that each person in the Business belongs to exactly one OrgChartStructure instance. The OrgChartStructure instance corresponds to a department, division, or line of business within the Business. being structured.
The goal of a business is to engage in commercial, industrial or professional activities.
An organization is a social unit of people, systematically structured and managed to meet a need or to pursue colective goals on a continuing basis,
An identifiable entity capable of transacting business.
A collaborative structure is a grouping of interests from several organizations whose goal is to meet a need or solve a problem that cannot be solved by a single organization

The DefinesSegment association class designates a collection of people within the organization who belong to the OrganizationStructure element. At the model level, the instances of OrganizationStructure would represent the names and duties of the departments and committies of an organization. So the OrganizationStructure instance ientifies the department and the DefinesSegment association class identifies the people within the Organization who belong to that segment.

Definition: A logical element or segment of a company (such as accounting, production, marketing) representing a specific business function, and a definite place on the organization chart, under the domain of a manager. Also called department, division, or functional area. Source: www.businessdictionary.com
A Role is a kind of relationship between a Party and another element in a business architecture core model, or in one of its associated models (e.g. strategy, process, initiative)

ModelElement is the base abstract class for all elements of the business architecture meta-model. It provides each subclass with attributes that include a label, a unique ID and a description. An attribute is provided for a definition if the methodology supports or requires a formal definition such as an expression in a logic language.
This attribute holds a formal definition of the model element, typically in a logic language, when required by the architecture methodology.
This attribute is used to hold a description or informal definition of the model element.

This attribute holds a unique identifier of the model element. To the extent possible, tools implementing this meta-model should strive to retain this ID over changes to the model in order to facilitate differential comparisons of two or more models where the models are derived from each other via a change process. The meta-model does not specify whether the ID is unique to a model, to a set of versions of a model or any other scope of uniqueness.
This attribute holds a label for the model element. The label does not need to be unique.

The CategoryType class represents that root of a categorization hierarchy. It is a specialization of ModelElement rather than BusinessElement because it is intended for use by the business architect in the process of creating and maintaining the business architecture model and/or performing analytic work based on the model. These categories should not be used to model business concepts or categories; classes of the BusinessObject and Information packages should be used instead.

"Operating Entity" is an abstract parent class for a number of concrete meta-model classes that represent business concepts typically used to refer to business entities, concepts and activities that form the ongoing operations of the business. The ability to change is a key characteristic of human endeavors and is carried out by both executives and workers. Business change is modeled as a sequence of changes in an operating model - a configuration of the business that is assumed to be static between these changes. NOTE: the concept of an operating model as a configuration of versions of operating entities is not currently defined in the meta-model.

While Policies directly influence behaviors, Policies are often specified as rules about the desired state of one or more OperatingEntities. In many cases, it is not necessary for the architect to directly model the behavior that will be influenced, but it is necessary to record the relationship between the Policy and the OperatingEntities whose state is described in the Policy. This "influences" association allows that relationship to be modeled.
If the influenced Behavior is later added to the model, it must be consistent with the associations to the OperatingEntities affected by or performing that Behavior.

Affects is a high level association used to associate a Capability with an OperatingEntity when the details of the Outcomes affecting the OperatingEntities are not yet described in the business architecture model. These details must be compatible with the constraint of this association.
ObjectConcept is the abstract parent of InformationConcept and BusinessObject.

Capability enables ObjectConcept represents the notion that the business must possess the capabilities needed to produce the components of the product and to satisfy any entitlements created by statute or contract. This is a high level association that is consistent with Capabilities producing Outcomes that affect ObjectConcepts where there is a ValueItem for those Outcomes that addresses the purchaser's expectations as defined in the CustomerSegment.

This is a high level association representing the valuation of a BusinessObject or an InformationConcept by a ValueItem. In detail, this association implies that an Outcome valued by the same ValueItem affects the ObjectConcept in a manner that supports its valuation by the ValueItem.

Behavior is an abstract class that unites Policy and CapabilityBehavior. Behaviors are controlled by Deciders and influenced by Policies that are controlled by Deciders.
Behaviors are also InformationTypes. This allows a Behavior to be expressed in a document that is a BusinessObject.

The Category class represents subcategories in a category hierarchy. Subcategories are owned by their parent and should be assumed to be non-overlapping (disjoint). In addition, anything assumed to be the case for a subcategory should also be the case for the parent of that category.
The "categorized" association links a Category model instance to the model instance of any BusinessElement.

A RoleElement defines a role relationship between two or more ClassElement classes in the meta-model. Each subclass of the RoleElement class has a particular configuration of associations that define the role relationships between the RoleElement and the ClassElements that are the components of the relationship. It is necessary sometimes to allow the business architect to define particular types of relationships whose roles cannot be statically defined by the meta-model. See the BusinessObject and Information packages for examples of this alternative method of defining relationships.
The BusinessElement is an abstract superclass for all meta-model classes that are intended to model business entities, structures, concepts and behaviors.
This association specializes the corresponding association from the SMM. ModelElement inherits from MOF Class, so BusinessElement is also a MOF Class.
The localElement association links an Assembler model object to a model object instance of any concrete subclass of the BusinessElement class.

UML:Constraint.name: Business Object makes explicit Information Concept is about Business Element

UML:TaggedValue.value: This "is about" association is a high level association that can be used to connect an information representing BusinessObject to the BusinessElement the information is about. When the model is developed to include InformationConcepts, for each instance of this association, there should be an instance of the association chain: BusinessObject makes explicit an InformationConcept that is about the BusinessElement.
Business Object makes explicit Information Concept is about Business Element
The ClassElement represents entities, concepts and behaviors of the business. It is the abstract parent of all the concrete meta-model classes representing these aspects of the business.

Associations as classifiers: Associations may be N-ary (N > 1). An n-ary association is metamodeled as a classifier with an association linking the classifier to each participant. N-ary associations between Business Elements correspond to active or stative relationships and are typically labeled with verbs. To facilitate translating these associations into prose, the constituent associations are labeled with "case category labels" derived from the FrameNet project.
Classes marked as abstract (and having an abstract stereotype) are not allowed to be instantiated in a business architecture model.
Generalization: This metamodel does not provide generalization associations at the model level except where explicitly indicated by a meta-association labeled "generalizes"

An end-to-end collection of activities that create a result for a customer, who may be the ultimate customer or an internal end-user of the value stream. (Source: Ralph Whittle and Conrad Myrick, Enterprise Business Architecture: The Formal Link between Strategy and Results (Boca Raton: Auerbach, 2005). A value stream has one clear goal, to satisfy or to delight the customer. (Martin 1995) Value streams strive to achieve the triggering stakeholder's value proposition as stated in the name of the value stream. (BIZBOK p 138) A value stream is associated with ("delivers") some value propositions, one for each customer segment that is taken to be a stakeholder for the value stream. Value streams may be decomposed into ordered sequences of value stream stages, and value stream stages may also be decomposed into ordered sequences of stages. This allows the architect to control the complexity of value stream diagrams. The meta-model does not limit the levels of decomposition, but good practice is that decomposition of value streams should be limited to 2 levels. Note that a value stream stage is not a value stream and is not associated with a triggering stakeholder or a value proposition. Note also that a value stream is not supported by capabilities.
This composition association decomposes a value stream into value stream stages. The value stream orders the stages.

BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Benefits of BA and Requirements Alignment Value stream > establishes scope for > requirement Value Stream > clarifies how value is delivered by > requirement Value streams > frame the context for > requirements Value stream > enables reuse of > requirements Per Section: Requirements Alignment with Value Stream Requirement > is contextualized by > Value Stream Per Section: BA and Requirements Alignment in Practice Requirement > links to > Value Stream

The "triggers" association links a stakeholder to the ValueStream that is triggered by the stakeholder. A stakeholder may trigger the value stream directly (e.g. by asking to purchase an item) or indirectly (e.g. as in a loan default, where the triggering stakeholder establishes a rule or policy for triggering the value stream). The latter situation is called "triggering by proxy" in the BIZBOK.

A distinct, Identifiable phase or step within a value stream that has a unique name, entrance criteria, exit criteria, and identifiable participating stakeholders(s). (BIZBOK 6.5) A distinct, identifiable phase or step within a ValueStream that produces some ValueItems. For example, each value stream stage creates value for one or more stakeholders as it moves from left to right. While one can argue that a value stream to authorize a driver's license for an applicant is most valuable at the end state, consider that there is value in allowing the application to pass the vision exam. The applicant is informed that he or she has the visual ability to get a license and has additionally learned that his or her vision is not impaired. (BIZBOK p 140) Value stream stages are owned by a value stream or a value stream stage and are not shared. It will often be the case that capabilities will support several value streams. The trade audit capability in a bank supports both the bank customer's value stream and the bank shareholder's value stream by reducing mistakes and fraud. The business architect must model this situation by sharing capabilities between value stream stages. Because capabilities are often associated with significant business objects or concepts and represent compositions of more detailed capabilities, it is possible that the same capability may support several stages in a value stream. For example, a case management capability in an insurance business may support multiple value stream stages in the claims processing value stream. This usually occurs when the capability has not been decomposed into sub-capabilities to represent the different activities that occur in different value stream stages. Consequently, when a capability "supports" a value stream stage it is implied that at least one sub-capability of that capability also supports the value stream stage. NOTE: an instance of ValueStreamStage cannot be simultaneously parented by both a ValueStream and another ValueStreamStage.

UML:Constraint.name: Either ValueStream parent or ValueStreamStage parent must be null

UML:TaggedValue.value: This "contributes to" high level association links a participating stakeholder to a value stream stage. It should be used to capture a significant contributing stakeholder to a value stream stage when there is no capability map or it is not yet defined. The constraint of this association defines compatible expressions of details involving the capabilities and their support for the value stream stage,
BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Principle of BA and Requirements Alignment Requirements > bounded by > Value Stream Stage
This association decomposes a value stream stage into an ordered sequence of value stream stages.

An exit condition is a condition that must be achieved in order for the ValueStreamStage to have finished its work. The exit condition is fulfilled by an Outcome of some Capability that supports the ValueStreamStage. This association represents the linkage between the Outcome and the ValueStreamStage whose exit condition the Outcome satisfies.

An entry condition is a condition that must hold in order for the work of a ValueStreamStage to begin. Such a condition must be satisfied by an Outcome from a Capability that supports a preceding ValueStreamStage in the same ValueStream.
In the capability map, a Capability may need an Outcome (e.g. the availability of a part to configure into an assembly) that is produced by another Capability (e.g. Inventory Management) . The meta-model defines the Outcome satisfying the entry condition as an Outcome needed by a Capability that enables the ValueStreamStage. This definition of the association between an entry condition and an Outcome simplifies the meta-model, compared with the initial definition, because it avoids a reference to a preceding ValueStreamStage in order to select the Capablity that produces the Outcome.
This association is a high level association intended to be used in value/capability cross mapping when the capability map may not be complete. It is also used to simplify presentations intended to create understanding and drive decision making.

This association is a high level abstraction of the "enables" association between the ProductConcept and the Capabilities that "support" the ValueStreamStage. A ProductConcept relies on a ValueStreamStage when the ProductConcept contains ObjectConcepts that are enabled by Capabilities that support the ValueStreamStage

Capabilities enable a value stream stage in that the activities associated with the capability create outcomes that contribute to the value created by the value stream stage. This association models the concept of a capability enabling a value stream stage.

The judgement of worth, made by an individual or organization, attached to something tangible or intangible and attained in the course of a particular interaction with one or more parties (BIZBOK 6.5 )
In a value stream, a type of value map, value is defined by a value item, which is an abstraction that represents how a stakeholder receives value from the outcomes of one or more capabilities associated with a value stream stage. (BIZBOK 6.5 p The value item is an assessment of the value of an outcome produced by a value stream stage in the context of a Consumer Segment. The characteristics of the Consumer Segment determine the characteristics of the Value Item.
This association represents one of the primary links between the outcomes of activities of the business and the value perceived for those outcomes by a would-be customer.

This association is an abstraction of the concept that a ValueStreamStage adds a ValueItem to a ValueProposition. It can be used to simplify presentations where the linkage to the ValueStreamStage is not material.
BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Benefits of BA and Requirements Alignment Requirement > links to > Business (Value) Proposition

This is a high level association linking a value proposition to a product. If details are added to the business architecture, they must satisfy this association's constraint that the ValueItems added to the ValueProposition must value the Product or ProductLine.

UML:Constraint.name: must align with consumer of value stream

UML:TaggedValue.value: {must align with consumer of value stream}
must align with consumer of value stream

Adds is a class that represents a 3-ary association. The association models the addition of a ValueItem to a ValueProposition by the Capabilities of a ValueStreamStage. The three legs of the association are: subject - the actor that causes the addition; individual - the ValueItem that is created by the subject and added to the aggregate aggregate - the ValueProposition to which the ValueItem is added by the ValueStreamStage.

The BusinessObjectRelation class represents a business entity that is composed of identifiable parts. Most commonly this will be a physical assembly, but model instances of the class should also be used to represent aggregations of business entities that are causally or otherwise connected (e.g. an email sent over the internet, communicating radio stations). The model instance of this class is connected to the components through the BusinessObjectRelationRole class and the associations tied to it. This class and its associations form a pattern that represents an n-ary relation. The roles in the relation are identified by the model instances of the BusinessObjectRelationRole class. The same type of business entity may be included in the relation multiple times, but with different model instances of the BusinessObjectRelationRole class. The BusinessObjectRelation model instance is effectively a type if some or all of its components are types. The BusinessObjectRelation model instance is an individual only if all of its components are inndividuals.

BusinessObject is an abstract meta-class intended to represent explicit entities in the business. An explicit entity exists for a period of time and may be a physical entity or an electronic entity (electronic entities are physical). A BusinessObject is not an action or an event. BusinessObjects typically represent entities that are changeable. Business entities may lose a distinct identity during a change process. For example, a steel billet may be melted and cast into a set of axe heads. Not all entities have a distinct identity; e.g. a liter of water.

The BusinessObjectType class is intended to represent the concept of a set of explicit business entities that are all similar in some way such as the set of products of a particular type or the set of parts of a particular type. Many other classes in the business architecture meta-model (e.g. CapabilityInstance, BusinessSocialGroup) could be thought of as BusinessObjects. While this is correct, the meta-model separates these concepts. Consequently, the business architect must make a conscious decision about whether to represent a business entity type as a BusinessObjectType or as one of the other classes in the meta-model. A signnificant criterion for the decision to model a business entity type as a BusinessObjectType is to consider the importance of the business entity type in the value streams of the business. If the business entity type plays a passive or supporting role in these value streams, then it is likely best modeled as a BusinessObjectType model instance.

A BusinessObjectIndividual model instance should represent an identifiable, explicit business entity such as a factory, a specific retail store or an identified executive. Typically there will be just a few of these model instances in a business architecture model. Most explicit business entities will be modeled as instances of the BusinessObjectType.

The BusinessObjectRelationRole class is the core element in a pattern. This pattern represents the roles in an assembly of business entities that form a distinct entity. Structurally, the class and its subject and object associations is an individual relationship in an n-ary association identified by the BusinessObjectRelation. The model instances of this class belong to the model instance of the BusinessObjectRelation class. These model instances have a model lifetime coincident with that of the model instance of the BusinessObjectRelation class.

A Value Stream begins with a stakeholder triggering the first stage of the value stream and ends when a product or service, notification, a degree of satisfaction, or other communication is delivered back to that stakeholder (BIZBOK p.104) A triggering stakeholder is defined as a category of stakeholder that initiates a value stream for the purpose of achieving a stated value proposition. (BIZBOK p. 134) A triggering stakeholder may be distinct from a customer segment in order to model situations where one person purchases a product intended for use by another. The "agent for" association links these classes and represents the agency relationship existing between the two classes of stakeholders. The triggering stakeholder specializes the customer segment. Normally the architect will create an instance of the triggering stakeholder, representing that the triggering stakeholder and the customer segment are the same, The triggering stakeholder may trigger the value stream directly (e.g. by asking to purchase an item) or indirectly (e.g. by establishing a policy or rule that responds automatically to an event such as a loan default). The latter is called "triggering by proxy" in the BIZBOK.
A triggering Stakeholder may either be a Consumer Segment or act as an agent for a Consumer Segment

Consumer segment is a description of attributes and values which characterize the purchase threshold function with respect to a particular type of product or service. It is also a set of potential consumers for the type of product or service.
An internal or external individual or organization with a vested interest in achieving value through a particular outcome.

BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Benefits of BA and Requirements Alignment Requirement > delivers value to > stakeholder Per Section: Principle of BA and Requirements Alignment Requirements > based on clearly articulated > stakeholder perception Per Section: BA and Requirements Alignment Requirement > links to > stakeholder

A participating stakeholder has a defined role or responsibility within a value stream stage by contributing to the outcomes of the capabilities associated with that value stream stage.
An individual or group with decision making responsibility for a Capability that supports a ValueStreamStage is a ParticipatingStakeholder. This aspect of the definition of ParticipatingStakeholder cannot be directly shown in the meta-model, but it can be expressed as a constraint. The notion of a group with decision making responsibility is represented in the meta-model by the Executive class in the Organization package.
Likewise, an individual or group that performs important activities associated with a capability is a ParticipatingStakeholder. This notion of an individual or group with responsibility for performing activities is represented in the meta-model by the Worker class.

This association abstracts the notion that a ParticipatingStakeholder contributes to Outcomes of a Capability that enables the ValueStreamStage. It is intended for use when Outcomes have not yet been defined in the Capability map or in presentations when the Outcomes of the Capability are obvious.
This association captures the concept that a ParticipatingStakeholder contributes to the Outcomes of Capabilities that support the ValueStreamStage.

CapabilityConcept is an abstract class that generalizes the concepts that Capabilities need Outcomes and produce Outcomes, that Capabilities have varieties of specific behaviors (the CapabilityCharacteristic), and that Capabilities affect OperatingEntities. This class is specialized in the concrete classes Capability and CapabilityInstance.
Capabilities may result in an outcome taking place or happening.

A particular ability or capacity that a business may possess or exchange to achieve a specific purpose or outcome (Ulrich Homann, "A Business-Oriented Foundation for Service Orientation", Feb. 2006, http://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/aa479368.aspx - as of late 2017 this URL produces a not found error)

This influences association is an abstraction of the details of a Policy influencing an OperatingEntity, typically a CapabilityBehavior or a CapabilityInstance. Capability is not considered an operating entity because it is an abstract categorization of activities performed by a business and cannot be directly influenced by a policy. The association allows the architect to model the concept that a Policy influences behavior of a Capability without having to model the details of the behavior.
Also includes cases where a new capability is enabled.

This association is a high level abstraction of the "enables" associations of the BusinessObjects and/or InformationConcepts contained in the Product or ProductLine with the Capabilities that produce them. The associations is valuable for aggregating and presenting information about the performance of Capabilities that enable the components of a product. It can also be used when the detailed components of a Product or ProductLine are not yet modeled.
A specialization of "Affects" representing a non-transformational, non-destructive use of an OperatingEntity by a Capability.

ProduceOutcome is a RoleElement that is part of a pattern that models a relationship between a Capability and an Outcome that can result from performing the Capability. This relationship has arity 2 and connects a capability to an outcome produced by the capability. The relationship is modeled as a class to allow it to have attributes and participate in other relationships.
A ProduceOutcome represents the occurrence of an outcome.

Capability Instance is a specific realization of a capability as it exists or is envisioned to exist in the context of a given business unit, value stage stream, or other situational context. The CapabilityInstance represents a bundle of real or virtual resources that have the ability to perform the Capability that is realized by the CapabilityInstance.
Open Issues: Specialization of Capability Instance in a model Decomposition of Capability Instance Use of CapabilityInstance to identify a particular configuration of the service component of a ProductConcept (see Product package).
Specifies realization of - this role element associates a capability instance that realizes a capability in the context of a business.

Affects associates a business object with an outcme and indicates that the outcome has affected the state of the business object, e.g. by creating a business object, modifying a business object, changing an association in which the business object participates, or changing the environment in such a way that the interpretation of the business object is changed, or destroying the business object.

UML:Generalization.name: Capability Specification is informed by Requirements

UML:TaggedValue.value: Capability Specification is informed by Requiremen

An end result or final product that is a consequence of an event, action, or a series of events/actions. Because a capability is generic, outcomes are similarly generic in that they represent a class of specific outcomes of a particular nature. In the context of a grocery business, a generic capability might be the displaying of produce. The outcome of this capability would be that the produce is displayed. The produce is the generic business object, whose prior outcome state was that the produce was held in a refrigerated space.
Outcomes may be decomposed subject to the constraint that the Outcomes in a decomposition may not be temporally inconstent with each other

The CapabilityBehavior class represents behavior specifications such as procedures, process definitions, policies and norms. It models the "how" associated with Capability. The behaviors represented by CapabilityBehavior can be either real or planned. Capabilities and CapabilityInstances can have behaviors that are represented by CapabilityBehaviors.
NeedOutcome is a RoleElement that is the head of an association pattern linking Capability and Outcome where the Capability may not be able to be executed unless the Outcome is present.

The "Characterizes" class is the core element in a binary association pattern linking CapabilityConcept and CapabilityCharacteristic. This linkage represents the association of a behavior to either a Capability or a CapabilityInstance. A CapabilityCharacteristic would be associated with a Capability during strategic planning to define an intended new behavior for the Capability. During Initiative planning, the CapabilityCharacteristic would be associated with a CapabilityInstance that defines the resources expected to be needed by the CapabilityInstance when implemented. Open Issue: The concept represented by this association is not simple. In practice, the skills identified in the resources of the CapabilityInstance would need to be linked to the roles specified in the behavior descriptions of the CapabilityCharacteristic.

A CapabilitySpecification is associated with a single CapabilityCharacteristic and one or more Outcomes produced and needed by the corresponding Capability. Conceptually, it extracts and summarizes features of the CapabilityCharacteristic and Outcomes (and possibly selected CapabilityInstances).

A CapabilitySpecification is a declaration of features of a Capability, some of its Outcomes, some of its Instances, and a CapabilityCharacteristic that would be useful to determine deployability of the CapabilityInstances in a particular mission.

An InformationIndividual identifies a particular BusinessElement. It is effectively an information key for the corresponding BusinessElement. InformationIndividuals can be members of an InformationType. This allows the InformationIndividuals to be constrained to identifying selected categories of BusinessElements without having to create individuals for the BusinessElements. For example, a business may have facilities at four locations. The concept of a facility is modeled as a BusinessObjectType and corresponding a InformationType is about the facility. Four InformationIndividuals (e.g. Paris, London, Beijing, Singapore) are members of the corresponding InformationType

The makes explicit class is the core element in a binary relationship pattern linking an InformationConcept and a BusinessObject. Information can be made explicit by encoding it in visual or other symbols used to convey information. Information that has been encoded in this way is modeled by creating an InformationConcept instance to represent the information and a BusinessObject to represent the encoding of that information (e.g. as a printed report or an electronic data set). These model elements are linked by an instance of the "makes explicit" class and the subject and object linking associations.

InformationRelation and InformationRelationRole that it composes is a pattern that is intended to represent a relationship of variable arity between information concepts. It should be instantiated as an association with an arity determined by the architect. The InformationRelationshipRole class is instantiated to provide role names for each of the links in the association.

The InformationType class is intended to represent categories (sets) of information things. In English, such things are denoted by the absence of an article and/or the use of the plural. For example, the phrase "monthly sales by region" denotes a category of information consisting of sales amounts in a default currency for an unspecified month paired with an identifier for a sales region.

InformationTypes can be arranged in directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) such that all of the individuals that are members of an InformationType are also members of the supertypes of that InformationType. The "specializes" association, when instanced in a model, carries the property described above. In UML notation, this association at the model/instance level would be typically represented as an association with an open triangle at the end specified by the navigation direction of this meta-association.

InformationConcept is an abstract parent for the three concrete information classes that are used to model information types, information individuals and information relationships. It provides associations that are common to all three concrete subclasses:
  • that a BusinessObject may make an InformationConcept explicit (tangible);
  • The an InformationConcept is about a BusinessElement;
  • That InformationConcepts may be linked by an InformationRelation.

This attribute of the InformationConcept provides one or more URLs that should reference electronic documents which make the InformationConcept explicit. It is semantically equivalent to instancing the "makes explicit" association pattern, but does not require instancing a BusinessObject to model the electronic document. It should be used when the architect does not see a justification to model the document as a BusinessObject.

BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Benefits of BA and Requirements Alignment information > establishes scope for > requirement Information > improves categorization of > requirements Per Section: Requirements Alignment with Information Requirement > links to > Information (Concepts)

The InformationRelationshipRole element provides a unique identity to each of the links in a variable arity association instanced from the pattern consisting of the InformationRelationship class, the InformationRelationshipRole class and the "subject" and "object" associations. This class can be marked to indicate whether the role has the containment property (i.e. the object InformationConcept has no existence independent of the existence of the parent InformationConcept).
Indicates that the subject InformationConcept contains the object InformationConcept when true.

InformationConcepts are often "about" other business entities that are represented as BusinessElements in an instance model. This class is the core element in a pattern representing that relationship between a BusinessElement and InformationConcepts that are about that BusinessElement. For example, a value of a financial instrument is an InformationConcept that is "about" a BusinessObject representing the type of financial instrument.

A Business Social Group is a group of one or more individual persons who collaborate to achieve a specific outcome. A group may be defined to consist of a single individual as in the case of a singular executive such as a CEO. The group may exist or be projected to exist as the result of a strategy driven business transformation. When a group is defined but the individuals who belong to that group are not yet assigned or known, the group defines a role.

The BusinessSocialGroupRelationship represents associations of various types between BusinessSocialGroups. This association may be specialized to represent particular types of relationships such as organizational composition, formal and informal coordination and so forth.

The ExternalUnit class represents a group that performs or is projected to perform work required by the business. This group is not part of the business and the work is performed under contract. The ExternalUnit class and the BusinessUnit class should be disjoint as the first is not a part of the business while the second is.

The staffs class is the core element in a binary association pattern linking BusinessSocialGroup with CapabilityInstance. It represents the concept that the BusinessSocialGroup provides the human resources for the CapabilityInstance. Note that a BusinessSocialGroup may provide human resources for several CapabilityInstances. At present, the meta-model does not provide representation for other kinds of resources (plant, utilities, automation, information technology).

The BusinessUnit class represents a group that performs work of the business and is part of the business. This class should be disjoint from the ExternalUnit class. Note that neither the BusinessUnit class nor the ExternalUnit class is disjoint from any of the other specializations of BusinessSocialGroup.

A BusinessUnit may adopt a Policy. Typically this means that the Decider with responsibility for the BusinessUnit has decided that the Policy will influence the behavior of the BusinessUnit. This association represents the high level concept of both the decision to adopt the policy and the associated notion that the Policy influences the BusinessUnit. It would make little sense for a BusinessUnit to adopt a Policy that had no influence whatever on its behaviors.
This meta-model association represents the concept that a BusinessUnit owns (has responsibility for) a Product or a ProductLine.
CollaborativeTeam is a specialization of BusinessSocialGroup that is organized to address a specified set of issues and is disbanded once its work is completed.

Performance goals are an objective for a set of measures that are used to manage the operations of a business and to audit progress of initiatives. PerformanceGoals are a kind of Policy and can be set by Deciders with the intent of controlling organization behavior. NOTE: this class may be eliminated in favor of a measure defined in SMM.

A Forum is a type of CollaborativeTeam that is composed of business decision makers. It is typically used to resolve issues requiring tradeoffs among two or more areas of decision maker responsibility.
The "controls" class represents the ability an Executive (decision maker) has to change job assignments and work descriptions.
The Worker class represents roles in the organization that primarily perform work. Typically the extensions of Worker and Executive will be disjoint, but this is not required by the meta-model.

The Executive role is held accountable for the work performed by the Non-executive roles. This RoleElement is the core class of a 3-ary relationship linking together the Executive, the Non-executive and the PerformanceGoals.
The Decider class represents a role in the organization that involves decision making and leadership of a group.
The DeciderRelationship represents associations between Deciders. Typical DeciderRelationship types are subordination and collaboration.
The "sets" association linking Decider to Policy is a more specific representation of the concept that a "Decider controls behavior" for the case where the Behavior being controlled is a Policy.

The Strategy package (and Initiative package) represent potential or planned change in a business. The Value and Capability packages represent abstract modeling perspectives on the business that are useful for 1) consolidating groups of activities the significantly contribute value to a product, or 2) consolidating similar structures and/or behaviors occurring in multiple parts of the business. The other packages represent business concepts that are often called the "operating model" of the business.
Only the operating model concepts of a business are subject to change resulting from a strategy or an initiative. The Value and Capability concepts are used only for model analysis and presentation; trying to change them will not produce any change in the underlying business.
It does not make sense in a static model (one that is not intended for execution or simulation) to represent a strategy that intends to change itself or another strategy. Having a strategy model that can change itself implies that the meaning of the model cannot be statically determined.

The "needs" class represents a 3-ary relationship between an "Objective", a "Course of Action" and a "Rationale". The labels on the associations connecting the "Class Elements" classes and the "needs" class indicate how the relationship should be read as prose; e.g. "The course of action needs the objective because of the rationale". The "needs" class specializes the "Role Element", indicating that this class represents a relationship
The before and after model elements must represent the same operating entity in the business

A Strategy is a collection of Objectives and optionally CoursesofAction that are associated with the Objectives. A Strategy should be coherent in that it contains no irrelevant Objectives and all CoursesofAction are related to some Objectives.
Anticipating that a model may contain several strategy versions, Objectives and CoursesofAction may be shard between Strategy instances.

An "Objective" represents a desire to change something. In a strategy setting, typically an objective describes a difference between the way things actually are (the ASIS model) and the way things are desired to be (the TOBE model). Note: the metamodel does not currently contain a class that refers to particular models that are instances of the metamodel. Such a capability is not typically found in MOF metamodels but could be added by means of a circumlocution - a device such as a text string that is understood to identify a particular model. However, there are details to be worked out about whether the ASIS and TOBE models are separately named models, or versions of a single model (permitted by some modeling tools). In addition, a new type of model element would need to be introduced that is capable of acting as a local proxy for an element that is actually defined elsewhere.
A strategic objective implies a change in some value items, including the creation of new value items and new value streams to provide those value items. (BIZBOK 6.5 p. 22)

The "expected to result in" element represents a 3-ary relationship between a "Course of Action", an "Objective", and a "Rationale". The associations connecting the "Class Elements" to the "Role Element" indicate how to read this relationship as prose: "The course of action is expected to result in achievement of the objective because of the rationale."

The "Rationale" class represents an explanation about why the CourseOfAction is expected to achieve the Objective or why the CourseOfAction needs to have the Objective be the case in order to be taken. The "Rationale" class does not have detail in the meta-model; it is expected that the architect will use this element to record a prose rationale.

The "Change" class represents an initial and a final state of an "Operating Entity". Typically, the initial state would represent the current state of the business concept modeled by the "Operating Entity" and the final state would represent the state of the same business concept envisioned by the strategy "Objective". This class is a 3-ary association linking the strategy "Objective" to the "before" and "after" versions of the "Operating Entity". The meta-model does not currently support versioning operating entities, but this is an open issue.

A course of action is intended to produce business change that results in accomplishment of strategic objectives. The "Course of Action" class is intended to represent strategic actions that are part of a business strategy. In practice, once the objectives are determined, the courses of action may not be fully known. As the strategy is elaborated, the course of action may be constructed in greater detail and the details modeled with the self directed aggregation association.

BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Requirements Alignment with Products Requirement > links to > Strategy (strategic outcomes) Per Section: BA and Requirements Alignment in Practice Requirement > links to > Strategy

Scope: Focused on a specification only as it pertains to directing changes to a Product, an Object, AND enabling a Capability. Core Touch points:
  • Requirements - requirements aggregate (up & down) into requirements (at various levels of granularity and type)
  • Capability - requirements link to Capability Behavior since we are specifying "how" a capability will get delivered
  • Product - requirement evolves the Product at a conceptual level, which is reflected in one or more Product Instances. Requirements will specify how a product will change via changes to it's design
  • Object Concept - In almost every case, information is touched when changes are made, so it's important to influence include both Information Concept & Business Object
  • Information - Requirement is framed by the business glossary, which are the terms used to guide the information architecture. Information establishes the scope and categorization for requirements. We are linking at the Information Concept to address the above alignment.
  • Stakeholder - requirement delivers value to the stakeholder, while creating clarity tailored to each stakeholder perspective
  • Value Stream & Value Stage - Value Stream clarifies how value should be delivered by a requirement. Whereas Value Stage provides the boundary for what is included in a requirement. Value Stream also enables reuse of requirements across initiatives (projects). Value Stream also provides context for requirement.
  • Strategy - As strategy is somewhat amorphous, we link to the strategy concept to simplify the alignment and to ensure requirement aligns with the strategic outcomes
  • Initiative - Typically referred to as a project (but not limited to a project) defines the scope, objectives, and measures for success to guide the requirements used to specify the design for a solution (waterfall or agile).
Other considerations: We may want to discuss alignment (via Proxy) to other Meta Models as Requirements will be influenced by one or more of the following:
  • Requirement MM from IEEE
  • Change Management MM from (TBD)
  • Business Rules MM from ?
  • Structured Metrics MM (SMM)
  • Business Motivation Model (BMM)
BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Requirements Alignment with Products Requirement > evolves > Product
BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Why Requirements Alignment Requirement > achieves > objective
BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: BA and Requirements Alignment in Practice Requirement > aggregates > Requirement

BIZBOK Reference: Per Section: Why Requirements Alignment Initiative (Project) > supported by > requirement Per Section: BA and Requirements Alignment Requirement > links to > initiative Per Section: BA and Requirements Alignment in Practice Requirement > links to > Initiative (project scope)
"A course of action that is being executed or has been selected for execution."
"achieves" duplicates "expected to result in" in Strategy
Maybe redo this part depending on Strategy vs Strategy_1
Changes capability instance, Changes capability characteristic, and Changes outcome should maybe put elsewhere (Foundation? Capability?) than in the Strategy package if we want to reuse them here?
"achieves" duplicates "expected to result in" (see Strategy diagram)

Capability Increment represents an aggregation of "after" operating entities expected to result from an initiative. This aggregation is often used to create a radar map whose dimensions are aspects of the operating entities, such as progress in implementing a reengineered business process.
Capability increment is a configuration of a capability instnace and a capability characteristic that are the "after" elements of a "Change" defined by an "Objective"

ProductConcept is an abstract base class for Product and ProductLine. It provides conceptual associations that are common to both Product and ProductLine, such as being valued by a ValueProposition, having ObjectConcepts as components, and being owned by a BusinessUnit.

A "product line" is defined as a series of different products which form a group, all made by the same company or its business partners. The ProductLine concept is used to group a family of products together in a meaningful way. A product line may be based on common traits (e.g. life insurance), a targeted buyer (e.g. commercial product line). A ProductLine should define the rule used to group the products together.
Model instances of this association should be interpreted as asserting that every statement that is true of the more general ProductLine is also true of the more specific ProductLine.
A Product is the overall experience provided by the combination of goods and services to satisfy the customer needs. This meta-model class represents the concept of a product.

Products may contain other products. Composed products are often called "bundles". This aggregation represents the non-exclusive containment of some Products by a bundled Product. Note that a bundle may be nothing more than a discount and it should not be taken to mean that the components of a bundle somehow rely on each other.
Product may be seen to be complementary to each other.
Product may be similar to each other.
This association should be interpreted in model instances as asserting that every statement that is true about the more general Product is also true about the more specific Product.

An entitlement is a right to benefits that is granted especially by law or contract. An entitlement creates an obligation on the part of the seller of the product to provide the benefit defined by the entitlement on the demand by the purchaser and subject to constraints and exclusions defined in the entitlement.

The Influences class is the core of an association pattern that links a Policy to an OperatingEntity. The meta-model shows this class explicitly in order to also show that the Influences association model instances can be constructed into an inheritance hierarchy that distinguishes various ways that influences is manifested in business terms (e.g. as a regulation, a norm, a business vision).

Instances of this meta-class link instances of the influences role element and represent generalization. If X and Y are instances of this meta-class and X->Y, then the set of binary relations specified by Y contains the set specified by X. Each instance network in an instance model must be a directed acyclic graph.

Policy - A course or principle of action adopted or proposed by a government, party, business, or individual. High level policies, such as "be responsive to the customer" are often called norms.
Policy is considered to be a type of Behavior. A process or procedure will describe a pattern of behavior; a Policy will constrain that behavior. For example, a sales process may be described as a sequence of activities consisting of receiving the customer's money, transferring possession of the purchased item and recording the sale. However, a Policy may restrict this process for specific types of purchased items according to the customer's age.

This class is used to represent a foreign model. It contains information needed to access the foreign model and perform the query against its contents (e.g. a filename string for an XML file that serializes a model and can be queries by means of an XQuery processor).

The externalSource association binds a query string to a ForeignSource model object allowing the modeling tool to find the connection to a query engine and pass it the query and the identifier of the foreign model to be queried.

The Assembler class provides two attributes in every instance: a query attribute for the query default value and a binding attribute for customizing the bindings between the columns of the result set and the modeler defined attributes on the Assembler instance object.
The modeler must be able to add typed attributes to instances of the Assembler.
The Assembler includes a method to cause execution of the query and a set of methods to manipulate a cursor on the result set. A side effect of changing the cursor is to update the modeler specified attribute values from the result set cursor position. On successful execution of the query, the binding is set to the first member of the result set. The modeling tool should be prepared to deal with query errors and null result sets.
A string allowing for customization of the binding between the columns of the result set and the modeler defined attributes of the Assmebler object.

This attribute holds a query string as its default value. The query string is sent to the ForeignModel object for execution against the foreign model, returning a result set if the query is properly formed and the result is not null.

The current metamodel has no association to show that the GLXModelNavigationSystem:CapabilityCharacteristic is "embodied in" the GLXModelNavigationSystem:BusinessObject. A capability can be realized by a CapabilityInstance. At the moment, a CapabilityInstance is effectively a group of human resources. CapabilityInstance could be redefined as any agent (entity capable of action). Some, but not all BusinessObjects are agents. The "realizes" association would need to be restricted in its target to "agents".

This class is defined in the OMG Structured Measurement Metamodel (SMM) specification. It represents a measurement whose type and attributes are defined by other classes defined in the SMM specification. Note that this meta-model anticipates making use of the forthcoming SNMM Version 1.2 specification.

This is a naive assembly of items from the Metamodel (copied from their respective packages), in an attempt to illustrate the meta model of business architecture at the top level as per figure 5.1.2 in BIZBOK Guide 6.5.
Of note: there is no notion of "Strategy", only "Course of Action" Organization (from DRAFT Organization_2 packages) is not well connected at all The connectors, for the most part, bear no resemblance to the original metamodel's connectors.
Based on this analysis, I think it's safe to plan for a top-level metamodel that is stand-alone, i.e. not making use of the elements as per this illustration.

This is a second, also naive, attempt to create a top-level metamodel. I'm showing only the packages themselves, not their content.
Value stream is part of Value Value stage is part of Value Objective is part of Strategy Business unit is part of organization

The Foundations diagram presents the basic classes that are specialized or used in the business architecture meta-model. The base element in the specialization hierarchy is the ModelElement. This class has four attributes that are inherited by every other class in the meta-model. The meta-model provides for the creation of any number of hierarchical structures that can be used to categorize BusinessElement model instances. A category hierarchy has a model instance of the CategoryType class as the root. The children are model instances of the Category class. Model instances of the Category class can also have children. The meta-model does not provide an intrinsic semantic for a category hierarchy. However, the children of any category should be non-overlapping (disjoint). And, if a BusinessElement is associated with a Category model instance X, the BusinessElement should be assumed to also be associated with all parents of X. Categories are intended for use by the business architect to mark business architecture BusinessElements in various ways during the construction and maintenance of the architecture model and its subsequent use in analysis projects. Categories are not intended to model features or aspects of the business or its strategies. For this reason, Categories specialize ModelElement rather than specializing BusinessElement. BusinessElement is the abstract base class for all meta-model classes whose model elements are intended to model concepts, entities, structures and behaviors of the business. ClassElement is the abstract base class for all meta-model classes intended to represent concepts, entities and behaviors of the business. RoleElement is the abstract base class for all n-ary relationships that represent relationships in the structure and behavior of the business. RoleElement subclasses are defined with associations linking them to the ClassElement subclasses that participate in the relationship. It is often the case that RoleElement subclasses are labeled with a verb and their associations are labeled with linguistic structure category names such as "subject" and "object". The association names, together with the verb of the RoleElement specialization facilitate reading the portion of the diagram containing these elements into natural language prose. See the Value package for an example. Strategy::Rationale is a BusinessElement, but not a ClassElement or a RoleElement. Conceptually, this class represents a potentially complex argument that supports a conclusion. It is used in the Strategy package to provide the business architect a way to capture the rationale of a strategy, specifically the rationale supporting the conclusion that an objective can be realized by following a course of action. The business architect can capture the rationale as a description or a definition attribute value in the business architecture model, or this element can be used as a proxy to an element in a separate, and more detailed model created by a separate tool. The meta-model adopts the OMG Structured Metrics Metamodel (SMM) for associating measurements with BusinessElements. The concepts, entities and behaviors of a business can often be distinguished by whether they are used to produce end products of the business, or whether they are used to guide the business and cause it to respond to changes in its environment. The former items are targets for strategy driven change. They are sometimes regarded as elements of the "operating model" of a business. The nature of the change, the expected outcomes of the change and the supporting rationale are represented by classes defined in the Strategy package of this meta-model. The OperatingEntity class is an abstract parent of all ClassElements in the meta-model that would be targets for strategy driven change. Note that the meta-model is being expressed in UML. The actual meta-model is expressed in MOF and the UML model undergoes a transformation to produce the MOF model. The subclasses of the RoleElement class, together with their defined associations, are a UML pattern for representing n-ary MOF associations. MOF associations are also classifiers and can be specialized, carry attributes and methods, and participate in other associations. Ordinary UML associations that are not part of a RoleElement association pattern are also translated into MOF associations. Finally, MOF does not intrinsically provide inheritance at the model instance level. Consequently, the business architect would not be allowed to specify inheritance between model instances when creating a business architecture model. The ability to indicate inheritance is introduced for specific model elements by an attribute labeled "generalizes". The instances of meta-model classes connected by this directional attribute should be assumed to be classes (i.e. they represent sets of individuals).

The ClassElements diagram documents the meta-model inheritance patterns for ClassElements. The OperatingEntity abstract class is the superclass for all classes in the model that are intended to represent business entities that are part of the operating model of the business. The significance of the operating model is that it's entities are the targets for strategy driven change.

Value is generated for a customer segment by a value stream. The value stream consists of a sequence of value stages such that each stage adds some value items to the value proposition of the value stream. The value proposition represents the value the business intends to provide, and usually does not completely satisfy the value requirements represented by the customer segment. For analysis, a value proposition may be matched with several customer segments in order to determine the degree to which the value proposition satisfies the value requirements of the customer segment. It is sometimes the case that a product is purchased by one person and another person realizes the benefits of the product. Examples include toys purchased by adults for children, and life and medical insurance policies. To model this, a triggering stakeholder may be distinct from a customer segment. In this case, the "agent for" association establishes the link between the triggering stakeholder who makes the purchase and the customer segment who realizes the value. The value proposition must be matched with the value requirements of both the triggering stakeholder and the customer segment. In the normal case, the triggering stakeholder and the customer segment are the same. Value stream stages also aggregate capabilities that are material to creating outcomes that provide the value indicated by the value items that make up the value proposition. The meta-model separates these outcomes from the value they provide because the same outcome may have different value perceptions to different customer segments. In the process of creating a business architecture, many details may not yet known. The meta-model provides some high level associations that can be used when details are not known. These associations are shown as thick red lines in the diagram. For example, a value proposition may value a product. As the business architecture model is detailed, the product contains a business object produced as the outcome of a capability that supports a value stream stage. The chain of model elements and associations used to model the fact in this statement represent the detail underlying the "value" association linking the value proposition to the product. The abstract associations shown as thick red lines have constraints that define the details supporting them. Tool vendors are expected to check these constraints.

BusinessObjects are passive, explicit entities in the business being modeled. The three concrete specializations of the BusinessObject supertypes are: 1. The BusinessObjectIndividual - a class that represents identifiable, individual explicit business entities such as a factory identified by its unique location or an executive identified by his/her unique role or title. 2. The BusinessObjectType - a class that represents a set of explicit business entities. It is not necessary to define the individual entities in a model instance of the BusinessObjectType; it typically suffices to define the type (e.g. iPhone 6, employee, assembly robot) without identifying the identifiable entities of the type. 3. The BusinessObjectRelation - a class that represents a business entity that is an aggregation of other business entities. This class can be used to represent physical assemblies; it can also be used to represent explicit entities that are connected together by causality or transformation, such as an email message sent via the internet. The BusinessObjectRelationRole class represents an identifiable role that connects a BusinessObject to a BusinessObjectRelation. The model instances of this class need not be labeled when the contained BusinessObject is unique in the BusinessObjectRelation (e.g. the screen of an iPhone). They must be named when more than one model instance of a BusinessObject is contained (e.g. the front and rear facing cameras in an iPhone). BusinessObjectType model instances may be arranged in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with the "generalizes" association. The meaning of this association is that if an individual is a member of a BusinessObjectType model instance B and A generalizes B, then the individual is also a member of B. This association may be thought of as creating a subset relationship between the set represented by B and the set represented by A.

A stakeholder is an individual or group (represented by the BusinessSocialGroup class) and inherits the characteristics of this class.
The Stakeholder diagram represents the two primary stakeholder concepts: 1. CustomerSegment - a stakeholder who receives value created by a value stream; 2. Participating stakeholder - a stakeholder who participates in the value creating activities of a ValueStreamStage.
In the case of the ParticipatingStakeholder, the diagram shows how this concept is defined in terms of meta-model associations and target classes. The base definition has the ParticipatingStakeholder contributing to some Outcomes produced by a Capability. Practically, these Outcomes will be substantial contributors to the value produced in the corresponding value streams.
Two high level abstractions of the "contributes to" association associate the ParticipatingStakeholder with a Capability (supporting the ValueStreamStage) and also with the ValueStreamStage. These associations are intended for use when the details of the capability map are not complete or when they are unimportant for the clarity of a presentation.

Capability Instance inherits from Capability so can produce and need outcomes. The Capability Instance is needed because a Capability can be defined only once in a Capability Map. However, a business may have or plan to have several instances of that capability. The design intent is to allow specialization of Capability Instances, Outcomes and Business Objects in order to add specificity to the Capability Map for both reference modeling and for individual business models.

This diagram shows some useful specializations of the "affects" class. Three of these specializations ("Assembled", "Modifies", "Derives") allow the representation of the source OperatingEntity that is transformed by the activity represented by these specializations.
This diagram is a draft to define the TOGAF notion of a CapabilityIncrement as a planned or actual change in a Capability, a CapabilityInstance, an Outcome and/or a CapabilityCharacteristic.

This diagram is a draft intended to capture the notion in capability-based planning that a capability can be described by its features that are used to determine the suitability of deploying a CapabilityInstance in a mission. The notion is represented by the CapabilitySpecification.

This diagram describes the classes used to model information in a business architecture. Information is used to make decisions, a mental process. Consequently, information is treated as a tacit (mental) entity in this meta-model. Information may also have an explicit (physical) representation in a business. Such a representation could be printed document or an electronic dataset containing visual or electronic symbols that can be interpreted by humans to transfer information. This transfer is known to be imperfect because: 1) the explicit representation is only a part of the tacit information; 2) the writer chooses the symbols and syntactic arrangements of them in a way that is to some extent personal; 3) the reader perceives these symbols and syntactic arrrangements in a way that is also partially personal. The same information may be made explicit in many different forms. And, not all of the information that is used in a business is ever made explicit. The meta-model provides InformationConcepts to model tacit information and BusinessObjects to model explicit representations of information. These model classes are connected by a relationship pattern modeled by instancing the "makes explicit" class and the "subject" and "object" associations that are part of the pattern and indicate the directionality of the linkage. Information is usually about some aspect of the business such as the location of parts in a warehouse, dollar volumns of sales transactions, amount of a price increase by a competitor. This is intended to be modeled by instancing the "is about" association pattern, consisting of the "is about" class and the directional linking associations "subject" and "object". The association pattern links the InformationConcept to the BusinessElement the information is about.

The Organization diagram displays the primary classes and relationships of the organization map. The organization package distinguishes groups and decision makers, but these concepts are not disjoint; a given individual may occupy both. The decision makers are represented by the Decider class. This class has three primary relationships: 1. Control - this represents the control a decision maker has over how work is performed; it is modeled by a relationship pattern headed by the "controls" RoleElement. This relationship involves the Behaviors that define the work to be performed by way of procedures, policies and norms. 2. Accountability - this represents the accountability the decision maker has for achieving established performance measures. The accountability concept is modeled by the relationship pattern headed by the "accountable" RoleElement. 3. Participation in decision making Forums used to resolve issues requiring tradeoffs between different areas of decision maker control and accountability for the benefit of the business as a whole.
The BusinessSocialGroup represents the human resources that have been organized into a team for some purpose. A typical purpose is to staff a CapabilityInstance. CapabilityInstances may have defined behaviors, represented by CapabilityBehaviors.
Deciders can be related to each other. Typical relationships are subordination and collaboration.
BusinessSocialGroups can also be related to each other in a variety of ways, including composition, collaboration and friendship. The architect should be careful when using this association to maintain consistency with other model details such as when distinct BusinessSocialGroups staff CapabilityInstances whose Behaviors cause them to act on the same BusinessObject or when an Outcome produced by one group is needed by the other. Such cases are effectively collaborations that are required by business operations and not collaborations established by choice of the groups involved.

Strategies are represented in the meta-model by the concept of some <u>Courses of Action</u> that are <u>expected to result in</u> the achievement of some <u>Objectives</u>. This expectation is supported by a <u>Rationale</u>, an explanation that contains arguments for and against the <u>course of action</u> and its expectation of achievement.
A strategy <u>Objective</u> is usually about a <u>Change</u> in some aspect of the business, represented here by the abstract class <u>OperatingEntity</u>. <u>OperatingEntity</u> has a number of concrete specializations, including <u>ValueProposition</u>, <u>ValueItem</u> and <u>Product</u>. A high level strategy will typically expect objectives that change the value of a business's products and services.
Strategies are rarely simple courses of action and expected objectives, and the meta-model can represent chains of <u>Courses of Action</u> with intermediate <u>Objectives</u>. In such a chain, a <u>Course of Action</u> needs one or more<u> Objectives</u> provided by a preceding <u>Course of Action</u>.
The meta-model also allows for <u>Courses of Action</u> and <u>Objectives</u> to be modeled as aggregations of <u>Courses of Action</u> and <u>Objectives</u> that represent additional details. For example, a <u>Course of Action</u> may aggregate a set of <u>Courses of Action</u> that are linked in a chain with intermediate <u>Objectives</u>. Some of these intermediate <u>Objectives</u> may be parts of a final <u>Objective</u>.
The meta-model will allow construction of strategy models that do not make sense structurally; the business architect is expected to use good judgement when creating strategy models from this metamodel.
The <u>Change</u> class is associated with before and after OperatingEntity classes. The before and after OperatingEntity class instances in a model should be conceptual versions of the same class instance. This constraint is not enforced by the meta-model and the meta-model has no notion of versions of <u>OperatingEntities</u>.

A ProductConcept is an abstract base class for both Product and ProductLine.
Product and ProductLine model instances can be arranged in a inheritance hierarchies where appropriate to facilitate the sharing of common concepts.
A Product or ProductLine is owned by a BusinessUnit. It would be typical for a Product to be owned by a BusinessUnit that is the same or a subordinate of the BusinessUnit that owns the ProductLine containing the Product, but the meta-model does not define this as a constraint.
A Product (or ProductLine) contains BusinessObjects or InformationConcepts. BusinessObjects are tangibles while InformationConcepts are intangibles. An example of a product containing an InformationConcept would be a license to use some specified intellectual property.
Entitlements are benefits that are contained in the Product or ProductLine and can be demanded by the purchaser after the sale. Entitlements are InformationConcepts and can be contained in a Product or ProductLine.
Any component of a Product or ProductLine must be enabled by a Capability in the sense that the Capability must produce Outcomes that create the component or create the benefit represented by the Entitlement. An example entitlement for an auto purchase would be a service contract; the business is obligated by the entitlement to provide an auto service capability to the purchaser, whether by the business itself or through an arrangement with a third party.
When the components of a Product or ProductLine have not yet been modeled, but it is desirable to track the Capabilities that would enable these components, a Capability may enable a Product or ProductLine provided that the Capability also enables some of its components.
A ValueItem may value a component of a Product or ProductLine when the BusinessObject or InformationConcept that is a component of the product is affected by an Outcome that is valued by the same ValueItem (see the Value package for details). This is a high level association that must be consistent with the details specified in the value and capability models.
For convenience in creating a business architecture model or in simplifying it for presentation, a ValueItem may value a Product or ProductLine if that ValueItem values some component of the Product or ProductLine. This association concept is represented by the valuation of an ObjectConcept by a ValueItem, because ProductConcept is a subclass of ObjectConcept.
Likewise, a ValueProposition may be said to value a Product or ProductLine when that ValueProposition contains ValueItems that value the ProductConcept.

The Policy diagram shows the generalization and other key relationships in which the Policy class participates. A business policy is a course or principle of action that is adopted by a business.
A Policy is a type of InformationConcept. InformationConcepts are intangibles that frequently have one or more tangible forms. Policy inherits this characteristic from Behavior (see the Foundations package).
Policy is set within a business by a Decider (an individual or group with decision making responsibility). Policy may also be set outside of a business by governments, regulatory agencies or market participation agreements. This is represented in the meta-model by the "sets" association between the general notion of a Decider and the Policy.
Policies generally influence Behaviors. associated with a Capability. Capability behaviors are represented by the CapabilityBehavior meta-model class. Policy is a component of CapabilityBehavior, along with procedure and activity specifications.
Policies are also said to influence elements of the operating model of a business. While policy directly constrains behavior of people and their automation agents, policy can also constrain behaviors indirectly by controlling the characteristics of Outcomes or the configuration and resources of a business, the meta-model provides a representation of this aspect of Policy through the "influences" association with any OperatingEntity.
To some extend, the influence of a policy overlaps the direct behavior specification through the CapabilityBehavior. The business architect will generally use the "influences" association when the policy constrains behavior indirectly and the CapabilityBehavior association when the policy constrains behavior directly.
A Policy may comprise a number of sub-policies. A particular policy or sub-policy may have both direct and indirect constraints on behavior. Because Policy is a specialization of InformationConcept, Policies may be related to each other by a variety of InformationRelationshipRoles.

When Assembler is realized in a model, the modeler can add properties to the Assembler object (as one would do for a UML class). The modeler can also specify a query as the default value for the query attribute of the object.
The modeling tool should provide a means for the modeler to invoke the query, retrieve the result set and bind the first member of the result set to the modeler defined attributes. In the absence of a binding string, the modeling tool should match the item names (or aliases) of the query (and result set) to the attribute names. A binding string may be used to customize this binding (the syntax of this string is undefined at present).
An Assembler instance may not stand alone in the business architecture model; it must be associated to a BusinessElement by means of the "localElement" association.
